name: Costa Rica
avoid: September and October are the wettest months on much of the Pacific side, so Manuel Antonio, Monteverde drives, and Tamarindo surf plans are more vulnerable to washouts.
Christmas, New Year, Easter week, and late-January dry-season weekends push up eco-lodge rates and shared-shuttle prices fast.
Driving times look short on the map but mountain roads and one-lane bridges slow almost every transfer, so avoid overstuffing the route.
stopoverNote: San José is the easiest hub for stitching together the Pacific coast, Arenal-Monteverde highlands, and the Caribbean side with shuttles, buses, or quick SANSA hops.

cities.name: San José
cities.notes: Capital gateway for museums, coffee culture, barrio food walks, and the simplest launch point for nationwide buses, shuttles, and SANSA domestic flights.

cities.name: Arenal
cities.notes: La Fortuna and the Arenal Volcano area combine lava-field trails, hanging bridges, hot springs, and easy wildlife spotting.

cities.name: Monteverde
cities.notes: Cloud-forest ridge town for reserve walks, hummingbirds, hanging bridges, and cool highland nights.

cities.name: Manuel Antonio
cities.notes: Quepos-Manuel Antonio base for Costa Rica's most famous national park, monkey-filled beaches, and easy sunset catamaran trips.

cities.name: Tamarindo
cities.notes: North Pacific surf town with beginner-friendly waves, lively dining, and quick access to Playa Avellanas and Las Baulas estuary tours.

cities.name: Puerto Viejo
cities.notes: Relaxed Caribbean coast base for bike rides to Punta Uva, Cahuita day trips, reggae bars, and Afro-Caribbean food.

itinerary.sim: Kolbi or Claro prepaid data plans are the simplest local options — expect roughly ₹1,000–₹1,500 for around 8–12GB. Coverage is solid in San José and La Fortuna, but it weakens on Monteverde roads, inside parks, and on stretches south of Puerto Viejo, so download offline maps.

San José.name: San José
San José.note: Stay in Barrio Escalante, Amón, or near downtown museums if you only need a short stop; Uber is usually easier than street taxis for evening dinners.

activities.name: Teatro Nacional guided visit
Museo del Oro Precolombino
Mercado Central snack loop for empanadas, casados, and coffee
Plaza de la Cultura and Avenida Central walk
Museo del Jade
Parque Nacional and Museo Nacional gardens
Barrio Escalante coffee and craft-beer crawl
Optional afternoon at Mercado Urbano TBS food hall

Arenal.name: Arenal
Arenal.note: Use La Fortuna as your base, start trails early for clearer volcano views, and pre-book hot springs if you want evening entry in dry season.

activities.name: Arenal Volcano National Park entry
activities.tip: Foreign-adult SINAC pricing is about US$15 plus tax; card payment is standard.

activities.name: Las Coladas and El Ceibo trail loop
Arenal 1968 lookout or lava-field viewpoints
La Fortuna town dinner after sunset
Mistico Arenal Hanging Bridges Park
La Fortuna Waterfall entry
Free hot-river stop near El Choyin or paid hot-springs session
Night walk for frogs and insects

days.theme: Safari Float or Sloth Trail Day

activities.name: Peñas Blancas safari float
Bogarin Trail sloth walk
Chocolate or coffee tour near La Fortuna
Hot-springs evening if skipped earlier

Monteverde.name: Monteverde
Monteverde.note: Even in dry season the cloud forest runs cool and windy, so carry a rain shell and avoid arriving after dark if you are not self-driving.

activities.name: Monteverde Cloud Forest Biological Reserve entry
activities.tip: Foreign-adult admission is around US$26; go early for the best birding and quieter trails.

activities.name: Sendero Bosque Nuboso and La Ventana viewpoints
Hummingbird Gallery by the reserve entrance
Orchid Garden or Bat Jungle add-on
Curi-Cancha Reserve entry
Selvatura hanging bridges
Coffee, chocolate, and sugar-cane tour
Guided night walk for frogs, tarantulas, and kinkajous

Manuel Antonio.name: Manuel Antonio
Manuel Antonio.note: Book park tickets online before you arrive, stay on the hill or near Playa Espadilla if you want buses over taxis, and carry water because the park gets hot fast.

activities.name: Manuel Antonio National Park entry
activities.tip: Foreign-adult SINAC entry is about US$18.08 and usually sells out in peak dry-season weeks.

activities.name: Punta Catedral trail and park beaches
Official guided wildlife walk at the park entrance
Public bus between Quepos and the park
Morning catamaran and snorkel from Quepos marina
Playa Biesanz swim or paddleboard rental
Marina Pez Vela boardwalk and sunset drinks
Quepos farmers market if your timing lines up
Rainmaker Conservation Park day pass
Suspension bridges and waterfall pools at Rainmaker
Afternoon at Playa Espadilla
Taxi or shuttle to Quepos marina district

Tamarindo.name: Tamarindo
Tamarindo.note: Dry season sunsets are the draw here, but book surf lessons and weekend rooms early because Guanacaste fills fast from December to Easter.

activities.name: Group surf lesson with board rental
Tamarindo beach and estuary boardwalk
Las Baulas estuary boat tour
Sunset drinks on the main beach
Catalina Islands snorkel or beginner dive trip
Playa Avellanas beach break by shuttle or taxi
Bike or golf-cart style local cruising around town
Night market or live-music bar hop

Puerto Viejo.name: Puerto Viejo
Puerto Viejo.note: A bike is the easiest way to move between beaches, but carry cash for small bars and remember that Caribbean weather can differ from the Pacific even in shoulder months.

days.theme: Cocles, Punta Uva & Jaguar Rescue Center

activities.name: Beach bike rental toward Playa Cocles and Punta Uva
Jaguar Rescue Center guided visit
Punta Uva swimming and mirador stops
Evening reggae bars in central Puerto Viejo
MEPE or local bus to Cahuita
Cahuita National Park recommended donation entry
Cahuita snorkeling tour if seas are calm
Playa Negra sunset back in Puerto Viejo

connections.method: Interbus shared shuttles are the easiest door-to-door option, Transportes San Carlos public buses from Terminal 7-10 are the cheapest, and SANSA flights to La Fortuna save most of the day if road time matters
The classic jeep-boat-jeep transfer across Lake Arenal is the best time saver, while Interbus and similar shuttles run the full road route if you prefer to stay on one vehicle
Interbus and other shared shuttles link Santa Elena to Quepos in about 5–6 hours, while public buses work with changes via Puntarenas or San José if you are trading time for cost
A shared shuttle is the cleanest one-day move, but if the road feels too long you can split it with a SANSA hop from nearby Quepos via San José or Liberia
Expect a long cross-country transfer by shared shuttle, or break it with a SANSA flight back toward San José and continue onward by Caribe Shuttle or MEPE bus to the Caribbean coast

extras: December to April is the cleanest weather window for this exact loop, especially if Manuel Antonio beaches and Monteverde viewpoints are priorities.
Buy Manuel Antonio park tickets online in advance and keep your passport details handy because peak dry-season dates do sell out.
Arenal and Monteverde nights run cooler than the coast, so pack one light fleece even if the rest of your bag is tropical.
Public buses are reliable but slow; shared shuttles like Interbus and Caribe are worth the premium when you are changing coasts or moving with luggage.
SANSA domestic flights are most useful for San José, La Fortuna, Quepos, and Tamarindo, but baggage limits are stricter than on international carriers.
Costa Rica is card-friendly, yet small sodas, beach rentals, and some bus counters still work better with colones in cash.
